coredata.dc:title: An ultrasensitive biosensor for virulence ompA gene of Cronobacter sakazakii based on boron doped carbon quantum dots-AuNPs nanozyme and exonuclease III-assisted target-recycling strategy
coredata.dc:creator: Cao, Xiaodong
Liu, Minli
Lu, Jie
Lv, Hongge
Han, Jinzhe
He, Shudong
Ye, Yongkang
Chen, Xiangyang
Wei, Zhaojun
Zheng, Haisong
coredata.dc:description: This work presented an electrochemical biosensor for the detection of virulence outer membrane protein A (ompA) gene of Cronobacter sakazakii (C. sakazakii), which was based on the mimic peroxidase activity of boron doped quantum dots-Au nanoparticles (BQDs-AuNPs) and a signal amplification strategy of exonuclease III (Exo III)-assisted target-recycling (EATR). The electrochemical signal was come from the electrochemical reduction of H2O2 by BQDs-AuNPs nanozyme. Due to the enhanced peroxidase-mimic electrocatalytic efficiency of BQDs-AuNPs and the EATR strategy, the biosensor showed a broad linear range (1.0 × 10-15 – 1.0 × 10-8 mol L-1) and a low limit of detection (LOD, 4.0 × 10-17 mol L-1). The constructed biosensor could also be applied in direct detection of extracted DNA from C. sakazakii. A good linear relationship (7.8 – 7.8 × 106 CFU mL−1) between the logarithm concentration of C. sakazakii and electrochemical signal was obtained with a LOD of 2.6 CFU mL−1. The biosensor was applied in the detection of impA gene segments in contaminated infant formula with recoveries ranged in 83.4 – 108.2%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemical biosensor
Cronobacter sakazakii
OmpA gene
Mimic peroxidase activity
Boron doped carbon quantum dots-gold nanoparticles
Exonuclease III-assisted target-recycling

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of the target ompA gene sensor
Preparation of DNA samples and PCR products
The sensing principle and feasibility of the biosensing method
Direct detection of C. Sakazakii in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful for the financial support from the Anhui Provincial Science and Technology Major Project (No. 201903b06020007, 202003a06020018 and 202103a07020003), the Anhui Key Research and Development Program (No. 20202a07020038 and 202104a06020008) and the Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province (Grant No. 1908085MC98).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fabrication process and detection principle of electrochemical biosensor.
(A) Photoluminescence spectra of BQDs and BQDs-AuNPs. The spectra were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm. (B) UV–vis spectra of BQDs and BQDs-AuNPs.

(A) XPS survey of BQDs. (B) XPS survey of BQDs-AuNPs. Inset is the XPS spectrum of B1s in BQDs-AuNPs. (C) XPS survey of cDNA-BQDs-AuNPs. (D) XPS spectrum of B1s in cDNA-BQDs-AuNPs. (E) XPS spectrum of P2p in cDNA-BQDs-AuNPs. (F) XPS spectrum of Au4f in cDNA-BQDs-AuNPs.

(A) and (B) are the CVs and EIS plots obtained on various electrodes in 5.0 mmol L-1 [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− containing 0.1 M KCl. (a) bare AuE, (b) AuNPs/AuE, (c) SA/AuNPs/Aude, (d) nanotags/SA/AuNPs/AuE, (e) BSA/nanotags/SA/AuNPs/AuE, and (f) after the BSA/nanotags/SA/AuNPs/AuE incubating in the mixture of tDNA (10 nM) and Exo III (8 U). (C) and (D) The CV and i-t responses of the fabricated biosensor towards 0, 1 nmol L-1, and 10 nmol L-1 tDNA.

(A) The amperometric responses of the biosensor towards various concentrations of ompA gene. From top to bottom are the i-t curves of detecting 0, 1.0 × 10-15, 1.0 × 10-14, 1.0 × 10-13, 1.0 × 10-12, 1.0 × 10-11, 1.0 × 10-10, 1.0 × 10-9, and 1.0 × 10-8 mol L-1 ompA gene. (B) The linear relationship between ΔI and the logarithm concentration of tDNA. (C) The amperometric responses of the biosensor towards various concentrations of ompA gene segments in total DNA extracted from different concentrations of C. sakazakii. From top to bottom are the i-t curves of detecting 0, 7.8, 78, 7.8 × 102, 7.8 × 103, 7.8 × 104, 7.8 × 105, and 7.8 × 106 CFU mL−1. Inset: agarose gel electrophoresis for marker (lane 1), ompA PCR products of 7.8 × 106, 7.8 × 105, 7.8 × 104, 7.8 × 103. 7.8 × 102, 78, and 7.8 CFU mL−1 (from lane 2 to 8, respectively). (D) The linear relationship between ΔI and the logarithm concentration of C. sakazakii.
Comparison of the different electrochemical detection methods.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: RT-PCR: reverse transcription-polymerase chain reaction; GO: Graphene oxide; AuNPs: gold nanoparticles; HRP: horseradish peroxidase; rGO: reduced graphene oxide; Thi: thionine; ERGO: electrochemically reduced graphene oxide. MB: methylene blue; BQDs: boron doped carbon quantum dots; EATR: exonuclease III -assisted target-recycling.

head.ce:title: An ultrasensitive biosensor for virulence ompA gene of Cronobacter sakazakii based on boron doped carbon quantum dots-AuNPs nanozyme and exonuclease III-assisted target-recycling strategy

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food and Biological Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work presented an electrochemical biosensor for the detection of virulence outer membrane protein A (ompA) gene of Cronobacter sakazakii (C. sakazakii), which was based on the mimic peroxidase activity of boron doped quantum dots-Au nanoparticles (BQDs-AuNPs) and a signal amplification strategy of exonuclease III (Exo III)-assisted target-recycling (EATR). The electrochemical signal was come from the electrochemical reduction of H2O2 by BQDs-AuNPs nanozyme. Due to the enhanced peroxidase-mimic electrocatalytic efficiency of BQDs-AuNPs and the EATR strategy, the biosensor showed a broad linear range (1.0 × 10-15 – 1.0 × 10-8 mol L-1) and a low limit of detection (LOD, 4.0 × 10-17 mol L-1). The constructed biosensor could also be applied in direct detection of extracted DNA from C. sakazakii. A good linear relationship (7.8 – 7.8 × 106 CFU mL−1) between the logarithm concentration of C. sakazakii and electrochemical signal was obtained with a LOD of 2.6 CFU mL−1. The biosensor was applied in the detection of impA gene segments in contaminated infant formula with recoveries ranged in 83.4 – 108.2%.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Boron doped carbon quantum dots-gold nanoparticles

ce:section.ce:para: Nanozymes are nanomaterial-based artificial enzymes which possess both the unique properties of nanomaterials and the similar catalytic efficiency and enzymatic reaction kinetics over natural enzymes (Sun, Zhou, Ren, & Qu, 2018). Furthermore, nanozymes also have advantages that general natural enzymes usually lack, such as good stability, low cost, easy preparation, and easy functionalization (Sun, Zhou, Ren, & Qu, 2018). Based on these properties and advantages, various kinds of nanozymes have been utilized in various fields, including analytical biosensing (Ye et al., 2020), clinical treatment (Mu et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2020) and detection, and remediation of environmental pollutants (Sun, Liu, Li, Qi & Si, 2020). Carbon quantum dots (CQDs)-based nanozymes, as a rising star in material science, have been applied in various fields. For example, the mimic peroxidase activity of CQDs (Tan et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2019) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) (Xi et al., 2016) has been explored for analytical biosensing with different detection methods like electrochemical (Xi et al., 2016), colorimetric (Zhao et al., 2019), and fluorescence methods (Tan et al., 2019). More importantly, the mimic peroxidase activity of CQDs and GQDs can be greatly enhanced by doping with other elements (Li et al., 2021; Zhao et al., 2019) or hybridizing with other nanomaterials (Xi et al., 2016). For instance, an electrochemical biosensor for cancer detection was developed by combining the excellent peroxidase activity of Pd nanoparticles (PdNPs) and N-doped CQDs (Xi et al., 2016). The biosensor realized rapid and sensitive detection of breast cancer cell line MDA-MB-231 by monitoring the reduction signal of H2O2.

Cronobacter sakazakii (C. sakazakii) is one of seven species of genus Cronobacter (Hu et al., 2018), which is an opportunistic pathogen that has been isolated from a wide variety of food (Pan et al., 2018; Xu et al., 2018). C. sakazakii in infant formula (Kent et al., 2015) can cause many life-threatening diseases including meningitis, neonatal bacteremia, and enterocolitis (Henry & Fouladkhah, 2019; Singh et al., 2015). Because of the deadly threat of C. sakazakii to infants, a standard of zero detection in infant milk formula has been established in many countries (MOH, 2010) and international organizations (EU., 2010; Fda, 2020).

Up till now, the usual methods for the detection of C. sakazakii in milk and dairy products are conventional culture-based method (Henry & Fouladkhah, 2019; Xu et al., 2018) and polymerase chain reaction (PCR)-based method (Qin et al., 2020; Zhu, Zhang, He, Zhang & Wang, 2018). The conventional culture-based method requires 3–7 days to obtain results because the whole detection procedure consists of enrichment culture, selection of specific colony culture and automation of biochemical identification. In China, one of the recommended methods for the detection of C. sakazakii in milk and dairy products is the PCR-based methods (AQSIQ, 2013a; AQSIQ, 2013b). These standard methods include a series of procedures for isolation and enumeration, target DNA amplification by PCR and determination or quantification of C. sakazakii. Meanwhile, there is still fundamental research on promoting new and alternative molecular detection methods, such as lateral flow assay (LFA) (Fu et al., 2018; Pan et al., 2018), enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) (Song et al., 2016), and electrochemical assay (Shukla et al., 2018; Yuan et al., 2020; Zhu, Zhao & Dou, 2018) for the rapid and accurate detection of C. sakazakii. To enhance sensitivity and obtain lower detection limit (LOD), some alternative methods combined with signal amplification strategies have been developed (Peng et al., 2019; Yuan et al., 2020; Zhu, Zhao & Dou, 2018). For instance, a sensitive electrochemical aptasensor for the rapid determination of C. sakazakii was developed by using an amplification strategy of methylene blue (MB)-anchored graphene oxide (GO) (Peng et al., 2019). This competitive aptasensor showed a wide linear range in 20 ∼ 2 × 106 CFU mL−1 with a low LOD of 7 CFU mL−1.

Exonuclease III (Exo III) is one of the exonucleases that has the catalytic activity to degrade double-stand DNA (dsDNA) from blunt ends, releasing 5′-mononucleotides from the 3′-end of dsDNA, and generating single-stand DNA (ssDNA) segments in the meantime (Wang et al., 2015). On base of this property, it has been explored as one of the signal amplification strategies, which has been widely applied in biosensing (Wang et al., 2015; Zhou et al., 2021). Enlightened by the unique properties of nanozymes, we fabricated an electrochemical biosensor for the detection of C. sakazakii in this work. The sensitive and selective biosensor was based on the enhanced mimic peroxidase activity of boron doped carbon quantum dots-Au nanoparticles (BQDs-AuNPs) and an Exo III-assisted target-recycling (EATR) signal amplification strategy. The sensing platform was constructed step by step (Fig. 1 ) on a gold electrode (AuE), selecting a highly conservative gene named outer membrane protein A (ompA) of C. sakazakii (Singh et al., 2015) as the target. As shown in Fig. 1, the capture probe DNA (cDNA) with biotin functionalized at the 3′-end and BQDs-AuNPs modified at the 5′-end was immobilized onto the sensing platform via the specific interaction between biotin and streptavidin (SA). While using the fabricated biosensor to detect the target ompA gene with the assistance of Exo III, the electrochemical reduction signal of H2O2 catalyzed by BQDs-AuNPs decreased due to the departure of 5′-mononucleotides with BQDs-AuNPs. More importantly, the decreased signal varied along with the varying concentration of the target ompA gene. With the signal amplification strategy of EATR, the lower the reduction signal of H2O2, the higher the target concentration. Therefore, a sensitive biosensor for targeting ompA gene of C. sakazakii was constructed successfully.

Tris-(2-carboxyethyl) phosphine (TCEP), 3-aminobenzene boric acid (3-ABBA), bovine serum albumin (BSA) were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Chloroauric acid (HAuCl4·4H2O), sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) and ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA) were obtained from National Medicine Group Shanghai Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Tris-(hydroxymethyl) aminomethane (Tris base) was ordered from Beijing Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Streptavidin (SA) and exonucleases III (Exo III) were purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Trans 2 K Plus Marker was purchased from TransGen Biotech Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). 10 × TAE and 2 × Taq PCR Mix were purchased from Shanghai Sangon Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All other chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further treatment. The buffers used in the experiment are shown in Table S1 (see Electronic supplementary information, ESI: Table S1). Ultrapure water was used throughout the experiment.
All base sequences (from 5′ to 3′) purchased from Shanghai Sangon Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) are listed as follow:

Capture probe (cDNA): SH-(CH2)6-AAT CAC GTC CGC ATC TCT TCT CCG AGT GCC GTT GTC CGG ACG AGC G-Biotin; target DNA (tDNA, ompA gene): CGCTCGTCCGGACAACGGCATGCT; single-base mismatched DNA (Mis1): CGC TCG TCC GGA CAA CGG C
T
T GCT; two-base mismatched DNA (Mis2): CGC TCG T
A
C GGA CAA CGG C
T
T GCT; three-base mismatched DNA (Mis3): CGC TCG T
A
C GGA CAA C
C
G C
T
T GCT; non-complementary DNA (NC):
ATT CTT GAA TTC ACC TAA ACC TAC
; primer F: CAT TGG TGA CGC GCA GAC T; primer R: TGG ACG GGA TAC CTT TGG.
C. sakazakii strains were provided by Technology Center of Hefei Customs District.

UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on a UV-4802 spectrophotometer (Unico Shanghai Instrument Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China). Fluorescence measurements were performed on a F97pro fluorescence instrument (Shanghai Lengguang Tech. Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were obtained on a ESCALAB250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) using an Al Kα (1486.6 eV) radiation at a power of 225 W. The morphology of the nanomaterials used in this work was characterized with a field emission transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100, Japan) at an accelerate voltage of 200 kV. The PCR reaction was conducted on TC-96/T/H PCR Amplifier (Hangzhou Bioer Technology Co., Ltd., Hangzhou, China).

Electrochemical measurements were performed on a CHI 832D workstation (Shanghai Chenhua, China) with a traditional three-electrode configuration. In this system, a modified AuE (1 mm in diameter) was used as the working electrode, a platinum wire electrode was served as the counter electrode, and an Ag/AgCl electrode (saturated with KCl) was used as the reference electrode.

AuNPs were prepared according to previous work with some modifications (L. Sun et al., 2017). Typically, an aqueous solution of chitosan (50–800 mpa, 10 mg mL−1, 1 mL) was thoroughly mixed with an aqueous solution of HAuCl4 (0.2 mg mL−1, 1 mL) by sonication. The mixture was then heated on a shaker at 60 ℃ for 1 h to obtain wine-red AuNPs. The prepared AuNPs have spherical structures (see ESI Fig. S1
A) with diameters ranging from 1.4 to 5.7 nm (see ESI Fig. S1
B). The prepared AuNPs were stored at 4 ℃ for later use.

BQDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method according to previous work with some modifications (Hu et al., 2020). Briefly, 1.37 g of 3-ABBA was dissolved in 100 mL ultrapure water and sonicated for 30 min. Then, the solution was transferred to a Teflon autoclave and kept at 200 ℃ for 10 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 30 min, and the supernatant was collected to be dialyzed for 24 h. Finally, the obtained BQDs were stored at 4 ℃ for later use.

BQDs-AuNPs were synthesized according to Kiran’s work (Kiran et al., 2019) with slight modification. The above prepared BQDs (9 mL) were mixed with an aqueous HAuCl4 solution (450 μL, 1.0 mg mL−1) thoroughly with a vortex mixer. After adjusting the pH to 10.0, the mixture was heated at 70 ℃ for 1 h under continuously shaking. The obtained wine-colored mixture was centrifuged, and the precipitate was washed with water several times. The purified BQDs-AuNPs were finally dispersed in PBS solution (300 μL, pH 7.4) and stored at 4 ℃ for later use.

BQDs-AuNPs were biofunctionalized with cDNA via covalent bonding (Ye et al., 2019). Typically, cDNA (10 μmol L-1, 50 μL) was denatured at 95 ℃ for 10 min and then transferred immediately into an ice bath for 10 min to form a stable hairpin structure. The solution of annealed cDNA was mixed with TCEP (10.0 mmol L-1, 10 μL), followed with keeping at 4 ℃ for 1 h to activate the 5′-end of the hairpin structure. The treated cDNA was subsequently mixed with BQDs-AuNPs (300 μL) and shaken for 12 h at 4 ℃. It was then aged in Tris-HCl buffer (10.0 mmol L-1 NaCl, pH 7.0) for 24 h. The mixture was afterwards centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 20 min, and the precipitate was collected and washed with ultrapure water several times. The obtained cDNA-BQDs-AuNPs nanoprobes were dispersed in PBS (0.1 mol L-1 pH 7.4) for later use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the target ompA gene sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The fabrication process of the DNA biosensor is shown in Fig. 1. It was fabricated on the surface of AuE. Before modification, a bare AuE was polished with 1.0, 0.3 and 0.05 μm α-Al2O3 slurry in turn to obtain a mirror-like surface. It was then ultrasonicated in ethanol and water in sequence, followed by cyclic voltammetry (CV) scanning in H2SO4 (0.5 mol L-1) within the potential range of –0.2–1.6 V at a scan rate of 100 mV s−1. After the CV curves were stable, the scanning was stopped, and the AuE was washed with water and dried under nitrogen flow. As shown in Fig. 1, the prepared chitosan stabilized AuNPs (5 μL) were cast onto the pretreated AuE to obtain AuNPs modified AuE (AuNPs/AuE). Subsequently, the AuNPs/AuE was immersed in 50 μL of SA (0.5 mg mL−1) at 4 ℃ for 3 h, followed with successively washing with water. The modified electrode was named as SA/AuNPs/AuE. Next, the SA/AuNPs/AuE was immersed in cDNA-BQDs-AuNPs (50 μL) and incubated at 4 ℃ for 2 h under shaking. After that, the unbound cDNA was removed by rinsing the modified electrode with 0.1% SDS and Tris-HCl (10 mmol L-1, pH 7.4) solution, and the resultant modified electrode was denoted as cDNA/SA/AuNPs/AuE. Finally, the biosensor termed as BSA/cDNA/SA/AuNPs/AuE was completed by immersing the cDNA/SA/AuNPs/AuE in 1% BSA for 1 h at room temperature to eliminate nonspecific adsorption.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of DNA samples and PCR products
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of DNA samples and PCR products was similar to previous work (Ye et al., 2019). First, the activated C. sakazakii strains were cultured in a bacterial incubator using Luria-Bertani liquid medium at 37 °C overnight. Then, the bacteria with different concentration gradient were inoculated on solid medium using spread plate method. After 24-hour cultivation at 37 °C, the concentration of bacteria solution was determined by plate counting. Second, the bacteria solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm at 4 °C for 10 min. The precipitate was collected and washed several times with water, which was then redispersed in 200 μL ultrapure water. The suspension was heated at 100 °C for 15 min, and instantly cooled in ice bath, followed with centrifugation for 15 min at 4 °C. Finally, the bacterial solution was diluted to a series of different concentrations containing the ompA gene segments and used as a template for PCR amplification.

The samples of the ompA gene segments from infant formula was also prepared according to Zhao’s method (Zhao et al., 2010). These samples of C. sakazakii (10, 50 and 100 CFU mL−1) contaminated infant formula powder were prepared in several steps. Infant formula powder (2.5 g) was added into buffer peptone water (22.5 mL), followed by the addition of cultured C. sakazakii to prepare three actual samples. Before extracting total DNA of these samples, proteins and fats in the milk powder suspension were removed by using chloroform. Briefly, chloroform (2.5 mL) was added into the contaminated suspension and kept shaking for 1 min. The obtained mixture was then centrifuged at 3000 rpm at room temperature for 5 min, the supernatant was collected and transferred into another centrifugal tube, followed by centrifugation at 12,000 rpm for 5 min. The precipitation was then collected and used for total DNA extraction as previously described. The prepared samples were finally stored in the refrigerator at 4 °C for electrochemical detection.

The PCR amplification reaction was performed in 50 μL of a solution including 5 μL of the extracted total DNA, 1 μL of forward and reverse primers (10.0 μmol L-1, each), 25 μL of 2 × Taq PCR Mix, and 18 μL of dd H2O. The cycling parameters were set as: pre-denaturation at 94 °C for 5 min, 30 cycles (denaturation at 94 °C for 30 s, annealing at 57 °C for 30 s, and extension at 72 °C for 1 min), and a final extension at 72 °C for 5 min. The PCR products were verified by electrophoresis on a 2% agarose gel at 120 V for 25 min in 1 × TAE buffer, and the electrophoresis bands were observed under ultraviolet light.

The electrochemical detection of the target ompA gene was carried out by incubating our fabricated biosensor in a mixture of Exo III (8.0 U) and various concentrations of tDNA (1.0 × 10-15 ∼ 1.0 × 10-8 mol L-1) at 37 °C for 2 h. After thoroughly washed with SDS, Tris-HCl and water, the resultant electrode, denoted as Exo III-tDNA/BSA/CDNA/SA/AuNPs/AuE, was used for electrochemical measurement.

The fabrication process of the electrode was characterized by using CV and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). These characterizations were performed in a solution of 0.1 mol L-1 KCl containing 5.0 mmol L-1 [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−. CV was conducted in a potential window of − 0.2 V to 0.6 V at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1, and the EIS was measured within a frequency range of 0.10 ∼ 10 kHz.

The detection strategy and the feasibility of our constructed biosensor were verified by CV and chronoamperometry (i-t) methods conducting in pH 7.4 0.1 mol L-1 PBS containing 3.0 mmol L-1 H2O2. CV curves were recorded in a potential window from 0 to − 1.0 V with a scan rate of 50 mV s−1, and i-t measurements were carried out at an optimized potential of − 0.55 V. The i-t method was also used to quantitative detection of the ompA gene under a working potential of − 0.55 V for 100 s in 0.1 mol L-1 phosphate buffer (pH 7.4) containing 3.0 mmol L-1 H2O2.

The morphology of the as-prepared nanomaterials that used in the preparation of nanotags was characterized with TEM, and the images are exhibited in Fig. S2 . As shown in Fig. S2
A and B, the BQDs were well dispersed with spherical structures ranging from 1.4 to 4.3 nm in diameter. The average diameter of the BQDs was centered at 2.4 nm. The image of BQDs in high resolution showed the crystalline structure with a lattice fringe distance of 0.33 nm corresponding to (002) facet of BQDs (Hu et al., 2020; Zhu, Shen, Zhu, Li, Hong & Zhou 2018). Fig. S2
C shows the morphology of BQDs-AuNPs. BQDs reduced AuNPs also displayed spherical structures with diameters ranging from 6 to 9 nm. Besides, we can observe from the image that BQDs were surrounding AuNPs in a specific region, forming the nanocomposite of BQDs-AuNPs. After biofunctionalized with cDNA, the morphology of cDNA-BQDs-AuNPs was almost the same as that of BQDs-AuNPs (see ESI Fig. S2
D). To confirm the successful preparation of cDNA-BQDs-AuNPs, EDS elemental mapping of cDNA-BQDs-AuNPs was carried out, and the photos are shown in Fig. S3 . All elements including B, N, C, Au and P can be mapped by this characterization method. The existence of N and P strongly supports the fact that cDNA was immobilized on BQDs-AuNPs via Au-S bond. Moreover, the maps of N, Au and P elements were of high-degree overlapped, further indicating the successful preparation of cDNA-BQDs-AuNPs.

The formation of BQDs-AuNPs nanocomposite can be proofed by using fluorescence characterization and UV–vis characterization. As can be seen from the figure, BQDs gave a strong emission at 425 nm at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm (Fig. 2
A, black line), and showed a strong absorbance at 295 nm (Fig. 2 B, black line) attributed to π-π* transition of carbon in the nanostructure. However, after using BQDs as a reducing agent to reduce HAuCl4, the fluorescent emission intensity of BQDs at 425 nm was dramatically reduced (Fig. 2 A, red line). This indicates that the emission intensity of BQDs can be quenched by the formed AuNPs. While in the UV–vis absorbance spectrum of BQDs-AuNPs, the absorbance attributed to π-π* transition of carbon shifted from 295 nm to 315 nm, and a new absorbance at 548 nm was found in Fig. 2 B (red line). The absorbance appearing at 548 nm is the typical absorbance of AuNPs. These results demonstrate the successful preparation of BQDs-AuNPs nanocomposite.

The elemental compositions of BQDs, BQDs-AuNPs and cDNA-BQDs-AuNPs were analyzed by XPS, which was adopted to prove the successful preparation of BQDs-AuNPs and the coupling reaction between cDNA and BQDs-AuNPs. From the XPS survey of BQDs (Fig. 3
A), the peaks at 532.08 eV, 399.69 eV, 284.78 eV and 191.58 eV were assigned to O1s, N1s, C1s, and B1s, respectively. After forming the nanocomposite of BQDs-AuNPs, a new peak at 84.58 eV assigning to Au4f (Cao et al., 2019) immerged (Fig. 3 B). Besides, the binding energy peak of B1s shifted negatively to 190.08 eV, indicating an interaction between BQDs and AuNPs. In terms of the XPS survey of cDNA-BQDs-AuNPs (Fig. 3 B and E), the binding energy peak of 133.39 eV was observed, which was assigned to P2p (Zhu, Shen, Zhu, Li, Hong & Zhou 2018). In addition, the binding energy peak of B1s shifted positively to 190.58 eV, while that of Au4f shifted negatively to 84.17 eV, manifesting the coupling of cDNA and BQDs-AuNPs. These results of XPS characterization prove the successful preparation of BQDs-AuNPs and cDNA-BQDs-AuNPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The sensing principle and feasibility of the biosensing method
ce:section.ce:para: The principle of the biosensing method for the target ompA gene was based on the enhanced mimic peroxidase activity of BQDs-AuNPs combined with Exo III-assisted target amplification strategy. Therefore, the mimic peroxidase activity of BQDs-AuNPs was first evaluated by performing a colorimetric reaction with the TMB-H2O2 system. The UV–vis absorbance at 652 nm was recorded in the colorimetric reacting system (1 mL) containing 25.0 mmol L-1 pH 5.0 PBS, 0.4 mM TMB, 25.0 mmol L-1 H2O2 and 10 μL of nanomaterials (see ESI Fig. S4 ). In the presence of 10 μL of BQDs-AuNPs, the relationship between the absorbance and the reaction time showed a linear relationship with the equation of y = 6.40 × 10-5 x + 1.80 × 10-2, while the equation of the reacting system containing BQDs was written as y = 2.21 × 10-5 x + 4.0 × 10-3. This means that the peroxidative oxidation sensitivity of BQDs-AuNPs towards TMB-H2O2 system was 2.9 times higher than that of BQDs, demonstrating good and enhanced mimic peroxidase activity of BQDs-AuNPs.

Before detailing the target amplification strategy or EATR, CV and EIS methods were first conducted in 5.0 mmol L-1 [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− containing 0.1 mol L-1 KCl for the characterization of biosensor fabrication. In Fig. 4A, a pair of well-defined redox peaks were recorded on each electrode. After modification with chitosan stabilized AuNPs (Fig. 4
A, curve b), the absolute values of redox peaks increased compared to those obtained on bare AuE (Fig. 4 A, curve a). This indicates that AuNPs can facilitate electron transfer during the redox reaction. Subsequently, the absolute values of redox peaks decreased dramatically after immobilizing SA (Fig. 4 A, curve c), nanotags (Fig. 4 A, curve d), and BSA (Fig. 4 A, curve e) onto the surface of the electrode step by step, showing the blocking effect of these substances on electron transfer. However, the absolute values of redox peaks increased notably (Fig. 4 A, curve f) after 2 h incubation of the BSA/nanotags/SA/AuNPs/AuE in a mixture of tDNA (10.0 nmol L-1) and Exo III (8.0 U). This may be due to the detachment of DNA sequences from the surface of the modified electrode. The results obtained from EIS characterization were in accordance with the CV results (Fig. 4 B, curve a - f). The electron transfer resistance followed the order of AuNPs/AuE (78.5 Ω) < Bare AuE (213.9 Ω) < SA/AuNPs/AuE (515.7 Ω) < cDNA/SA/AuNPs/AuE (642.2 Ω) < BSA/cDNA/SA/AuNPs/AuE incubated in a mixture of target and Exo III (745.1 Ω) < BSA/cDNA/SA/AuNPs/AuE (1425.0 Ω). These results prove the successful fabrication of the biosensor.

The sensing principle and feasibility of the biosensing method were verified with CV and amperometic methods, respectively. Fig. 4 C are the CV curves recorded on the fabricated biosensor under different circumstances. In the absence of tDNA and presence of Exo III, the biosensor gave no obvious reduction peak in pH 7.4 0.1 mol L-1 PBS without H2O2 (Fig. 4 C, curve a), while a distinct reduction peak was obtained in pH 7.4 0.1 mol L-1 PBS containing 3.0 mmol L-1 H2O2 (Fig. 4 C, curve b). Compared with curve b, the reduction peak of H2O2 (3.0 mmol L-1) declined in presence of 1.0 nmol L-1 tDNA without the participation of Exo III (Fig. 4 C, curve c). The reduction signal of H2O2 was further decreased after the biosensor was incubated in a mixture containing 1.0 nmol/L target and 8.0 U Exo III (Fig. 4 C, curve d). Furthermore, the decrease of the reduction signal of H2O2 correlated with the concentration of tDNA. As shown in Fig. 4 D, after incubating in a mixture of 10.0 nmol L-1 tDNA and 8.0 U Exo III and a mixture of 1 nmol L-1 tDNA and 8 U Exo III, respectively, the biosensor gave a lower reduction signal to the former (Fig. 4 D, curve c) than that of the latter (Fig. 4 D, curve b). This indicates that the higher the concentration of tDNA, the more the release of 5′-mononucleotides with BQDs-AuNPs, and the lower the reduction signal of H2O2.

In the absence of tDNA, the hairpin structure of the cDNA can’t be opened, and Exo III can’t degrade cDNA owing to the designed four base protruding sequence at 3′-end. After the hybridization reaction between cDNA and tDNA, the BQDs-AuNPs nanotags at 5′-end of the formed dsDNA were farther from the electrode surface than they were in the hairpin structure. This may be the reason for the decline of the reduction signal even without the assistance of Exo III. However, by combining with the signal amplification strategy of EATR, the reduction signal of H2O2 can be further decreased. This means that the hairpin structure of cDNA was opened to form dsDNA due to the hybridization interaction between tDNA and cDNA. At the same time, the formed dsDNA was subsequently degraded with the assistance of Exo III, releasing the ssDNA segments of tDNA and 5′-mononucleotides with BQDs-AuNPs. The released tDNA can continue to hybridize with the matched fragments in the cDNA, thus further triggering the degradation of dsDNA by Exo III and the target cycles. As a result, the reduction signal of H2O2 catalyzed by BQDs-AuNPs was decreased much more significantly than that without the assistance of Exo III. In a word, the above results prove the feasibility of our fabricated biosensor.

The analytical performance of our constructed biosensor was evaluated by amperometric method. Parameters that affecting the analytical performance were optimized. These parameters included the incubation time and temperature for hybridization and degradation reactions, the pH of buffer solution, and the working potential of the amperometric experiment. As shown in Fig. S5 , the biosensor exhibited the best analytical performance under the conditions as follow: the incubation time of 120 min (see ESI Fig. S5
A) at 37 ℃ (see ESI Fig. S5
B), the pH of 7.4 of the detection buffer solution (see ESI Fig. S5
C), and the working potential of −0.55 V (see ESI Fig. S5
D).

The analytical performance for quantitative detection of the ompA gene of the fabricated biosensor (BSA/cDNA/SA/AuNPs/AuE) was assessed by incubating in mixtures containing various concentrations of the ompA target (1.0 × 10-15 ∼ 1.0 × 10-8 mol L-1) and Exo III (8.0 U). Under the optimized conditions, the obtained amperometric responses for various concentrations of tDNA are displayed in Fig. 5
A. Obviously, the current response on the fabricated biosensor showed a gradual decrease with the increase of tDNA concentration. This indicates an increase amount of the detachment of BQDs-AuNPs from the surface of the modified electrode along with the increasing concentration of tDNA. Fig. 5 B shows the relationship between ΔI (the result of the blank response at 100 s minus the response towards various concentrations of tDNA at 100 s) and logarithm concentration. As can be obtained from the figure, the ΔI exhibited semi-logarithmic linearity according to the following equation (Eq.):
(1)
Δ I
μ A
= 9.84 ×
10
- 2
log C
mol ·
L
- 1
+ 1.68
R = 0.996
The LOD of the ompA target gene was calculated to be 4.0 × 10-17 mol L-1 by introducing 3 times standard deviation (SD) of blank signal into Eq. (1).

The analytical specificity of the ompA biosensor was further evaluated by using the fabricated biosensor to detect various mismatched DNA sequences. These sequences included synthetic non-complementary DNA (NC), single-base mismatched DNA (Mis1), double-base mismatched DNA (Mis2), three-base mismatched DNA (Mis3), and DNA extracts from Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Salmonella. The amperometric responses towards these sequences were then compared with the response to the target ompA gene to assess the selectivity of the constructed biosensor. Fig. S6
A shows the responses in histogram on the fabricated biosensor towards NC (100.0 pmol L-1), Mis1 (100.0 pmol L-1), Mis2 (100.0 pmol L-1), Mis3 (100 pmol L-1)), tDNA (10.0 pmol L-1), a mixture (Mix) containing tDNA (10.0 pmol L-1) and mismatched DNA sequences (10.0 pmol L-1 each), and DNA extracts from E. coli (1.0 × 103 CFU mL−1) and Salmonella (1.0 × 103 CFU mL−1). It is obviously that the fabricated biosensor gave strong responses towards tDNA (0.600 μA) and Mix (0.619 μA). This means that the biosensor shows little difference towards tDNA and tDNA mixed with other DNA sequences. Besides, the response of the biosensor towards other DNA sequences decreased in an order of Mis1 (0.294 μA), Mis2 (0.201 μA), Mis3 (0.097 μA) and NC (0.016 μA), E.coli (0.045 μA), and Salmonella (0.007 μA). The results demonstrate that the designed biosensor can effectively discriminate different DNA sequences and processed an excellent selectivity.

The fabrication reproducibility of the biosensor was also evaluated by constructing biosensors on different AuEs and using them to detect the same concentration the tDNA (see ESI Fig. S6
B). The relative standard deviation (RSD) of 2.7% for 10.0 pmol L-1 the target ompA gene, manifesting a satisfactory reproducibility of the biosensor.

The stability of the ompA gene sensor was investigated by examining the changes of ΔI after their storage in refrigerator at –20 °C for certain days (see ESI Fig. S6
C). After 3-day, 7-day and 14-day storage, the values of ΔI maintained 94.2%, 90.3% and 81.3% of its initial signal response, respectively, showing an acceptable stability possessed by the developed biosensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Direct detection of C. Sakazakii in real samples
ce:section.ce:para:
The practical application of the proposed biosensor was evaluated by detecting the ompA gene segments in total DNA extracted from C. sakazakii. Total DNA was extracted from various concentration of C. sakazakii using a hydrothermal treatment. The extracted total DNA was then used as template for target amplification by PCR. The band at 250 bp in agarose gel electrophoresis analysis (Fig. 5 C, inset) was assigned to the ompA gene segments. Fig. 5 C shows the amperometric responses on the proposed biosensor to the ompA gene segments in total DNA solutions extracted from different concentrations of C. sakazakii. Compared to the blank signal, the current response gradually decreased along with the increasing concentration of C. sakazakii (Fig. 5 D). In the concentration range of 7.8 – 7.8 × 106 CFU mL−1, there was a good linear relationship between ΔI and the logarithm concentration of C. sakazakii was obtained, and the linear equation is written as follow:
(2)
Δ I
μ A
= 0.551
l o g C
CFU
m
L
- 1
+ 0.068
R = 0.995

The LOD of C. sakazakii was estimated to be 2.6 CFU mL−1. The analytical performance in linear range and LOD of our constructed biosensor for the detection of C. sakazakii were compared with other reported electrochemical biosensors, and the results are listed in Table 1 . As shown in the table, our biosensor has a broader linear range than most of the listed biosensors, and the lowest LOD among these reported biosensors.

Finally, the fabricated biosensor was used to detect the ompA gene segments in total DNA extracted from C. sakazakii. contaminated infant formula (see ESI Table S2). According to Eq. (2) , the concentrations of C. sakazakii. detected in three samples were 8.4 ± 0.4, 41.7 ± 1.6 and 108.2 ± 3.4 CFU mL −
1, respectively. The recoveries of C. sakazakii. in the contaminated infant formula were in the range of 84.0%-108.2%. It demonstrates that the biosensor has applicability to detect the ompA gene in actual samples.

In summary, we have developed a highly sensitive electrochemical assay for convenient detection of ompA gene in C. sakazakii. The sensitive detection was based on the enhanced mimic peroxidase activity of BQDs-AuNPs to H2O2 and Exo III-assisted target-recycling strategy. The proposed biosensor showed good sensitivity to the target ompA gene with a LOD as low as 4.0 × 10-17 mol L-1. Moreover, the gene sensor can be applied in the detection of the ompA gene segments extracted from C. sakazakii with a LOD of 2.6 CFU mL−1. The developed biosensor exhibited excellent selectivity, good fabrication reproducibility, and acceptable storage stability, and its detection results for the ompA gene segments in contaminated infant formula were reliable with recoveries ranging from 84.0% to 108.2%. These suggest that it can be used as a potential tool for C. sakazakii assay in food safety monitoring and clinical diagnosis.

Xiaodong Cao: Methodology, Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Minli Liu: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Jie Lu: Investigation. Hongge Lv: Investigation. Jinzhe Han: Investigation. Shudong He: Resources, Writing – review & editing. Yongkang Ye: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Xiangyang Chen: Funding acquisition. Zhaojun Wei: Resources, Writing – review & editing. Haisong Zheng: Funding acquisition, Resources, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for the financial support from the Anhui Provincial Science and Technology Major Project (No. 201903b06020007, 202003a06020018 and 202103a07020003), the Anhui Key Research and Development Program (No. 20202a07020038 and 202104a06020008) and the Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province (Grant No. 1908085MC98).

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Ye et al., 2020
